title: The Arrival in Egypt (Monthly) — The Nativity of St. John the Baptist (Major Feast)
primary_saint: The Monthly Commemoration of the Arrival of the Holy Family in Egypt and the Great Feast of the Nativity of the Holy Prophet and Forerunner John the Baptist
narrative:
On the twenty-fourth of Sene, the holy Church celebrates two great feasts simultaneously: the monthly commemoration of the Arrival of the Holy Family in Egypt — that moment when the child Jesus, carried in his mother's arms and guided by Joseph, first touched the soil of the African continent and blessed it with his presence — and the Great Feast of the Nativity of the holy Prophet, Forerunner, and Baptist John, whose birth the angel Gabriel had announced to his father Zechariah in the Temple and whose leaping in his mother's womb at the sound of Mary's greeting was the first act of recognition of the Messiah by any human being. The coincidence of these two feasts on the same day is one of the most theologically resonant accidents of the calendar: the day that commemorates the Holy Family's arrival in Africa is also the day that celebrates the birth of the one who would announce their son's ministry, the day of geographical arrival and the day of the prophetic voice being joined in a single celebration that encompasses both the land and the herald.

The theology of the Holy Family's Arrival in Egypt — distinct from the First Entry celebrated earlier in the month — is a theology of the completed crossing: if the First Entry was the moment of crossing the border, the Arrival is the moment of settling, of the Holy Family finding in Egypt the refuge and the hospitality that Palestine under Herod could not provide. The tradition of the Coptic and Ethiopian churches has always understood the Holy Family's time in Egypt not as an interruption of the salvific narrative but as an integral part of it: the Christ who would be the saviour of all nations was sheltered by the people of the oldest of all civilisations, was nourished by the land of the Nile, was carried in love through the towns and villages of the Delta and the Upper Nile, and left behind in each place he visited a blessing that the tradition has never ceased to celebrate. Egypt sheltered God, and the tradition regards this as among the highest honours that any nation has ever received.

The Nativity of John the Baptist is one of the few birthdays celebrated in the liturgical calendar — most saints are honoured on the day of their death, their dies natalis in the heavenly sense, but John and the Virgin Mary and Christ himself are honoured also on the days of their earthly births, because in each case the earthly birth was itself a divine act rather than merely a human one. John's birth was announced by an angel, was accomplished through the miraculous opening of a womb that had been closed by age, was preceded by his father's loss of speech as a sign of the magnitude of what was coming, and was accompanied by the restoration of that speech at the moment of John's naming: Zechariah, who could not speak because he had doubted the angel's word, spoke again the moment he confirmed that the child's name was John, not Zechariah as the family had expected. The restoration of speech at the naming of John is the tradition's sign that the prophetic voice had been silenced for the duration of its preparation and would speak again only when the prophet himself had been named and given to the world.

The Great Feast of John's Nativity on this twenty-fourth day of Sene is the tradition's celebration of the prophetic voice given to the world: the voice that would grow in the wilderness, that would cry from the Jordan, that would point to the Lamb of God, that would rebuke Herod and lose its head for doing so, and that would be heard at the Last Judgment when John stands among the foremost of the saints before the throne of the one he announced. The Ethiopian tradition celebrates John's birth with a solemnity that reflects his unique position as the greatest of those born of women — as Jesus himself declares — and the coincidence of his birth-feast with the monthly commemoration of the Holy Family's arrival in Africa gives this twenty-fourth day of Sene a density of sacred meaning unmatched in the month: the land that sheltered the infant God and the man born to announce him are honoured together, Africa and the Forerunner meeting in a single celebration of preparation and arrival. Amen.
